html:
X

вАЬI speak of this, both from the pulpit, and to every man in particular.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭвБ†вАФThe wind put an end to the rest of the sentence by blowing violently down the priestвАЩs throat, making him fall into a fit of coughing. Antek was silent.

The gale was growing fiercer, sweeping down the road, lashing the poplars, storming through them, and causing them to bend and moan and shriek aloud with rage.

вАЬMan, I have told you,вАЭ the priest went on to say, вАЬthat I myself took the mare down to the pond.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Blind as she is, she may go astray in some coppice, and perhaps break a leg.вАЭвБ†вАФThe very thought made him turn pale, and he continued looking under every tree, and seeking in every field.

вАЬWell, but she always went about freely.вАЭ

вАЬShe knows well her way to the pond. Anyone might find a pail for her to drink from, and then turn her round: she would have come back by herself.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Valek!вАЭ he suddenly cried, thinking he saw someone among the poplars.

вАЬI saw Valek on our side of the pond; but that was before twilight set in.вАЭ

вАЬGone perhaps to look for her: a little too late!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ A mare twenty years old! She was foaled soon after I came here, and deserves to be fed for mercyвАЩs sake.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ As much attached as any man can be.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Good Heavens! if any harm should have befallen the poor beast!вАЭ

вАЬWhat on earth can happen?вАЭ Antek growled, in a surly mood. He had come to his Reverence to complain and get counsel; and he had been, not only reprimanded, but asked to seek the lost mare besides! No doubt the mare, so old and blind, deserved pity; but ought not a fellow-man to come first?

вАЬAs to you, you are to master yourself; do you hear? And curse him not! he is your father!вАЭ

вАЬOh, that,вАЭ said Antek very bitterly, вАЬthat I know well.вАЭ

вАЬIt were a grievous sin and offence against God. And no blessing will there be for him that in anger raises his hand against his father, to break the commandment!вАЭ

вАЬI want justice: no more.вАЭ

вАЬNo, вАЩtis revenge you seek.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Am I wrong?вАЭ

Antek was at a loss for an answer.

вАЬNow I will tell you one thing more: вАШA docile calf, beyond all doubt, thrives, sucks much milk, and waxes stout.вАЩвАКвАЭ

вАЬвАКвАШDocile!вАЩ The word sticks in my throat, I have so much of it. Shall I allow a man to do me every wrong in the world, simply because he is my father? Are children forbidden to seek justice for the wrong done?вБ†вАФGood God! if thatвАЩs the order of things, I had as lief bid it farewell, and go anywhere to get away from it.вАЭ

вАЬGo, then; what is it prevents you?вАЭ cried the priest, taking fire on a sudden.

вАЬWell may I go: whatвБ†вАФwhat is there left to me here now?вАЭ he muttered, almost in tears.

вАЬYou are simply talking nonsense. Others have not one bit of land: yet they stay on, and work, and thank God that they have work to do. You had far better settle down to do something, and not complain like a woman. You are strong and able, and have something to lay your hands to besides.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬYes, indeed; three whole acres!вАЭ was the ironical reply.

вАЬAnd a wife and child, who belong to you too: do not forget it.вАЭ

They were now in front of the tavern; the windows were all aglow, and from the road where they stood they could hear voices inside.

вАЬWhat! another drunken bout?вАЭ

вАЬвАКвАЩTis the recruits who were chosen during the summer, drinking to keep their spirits up. Next Sunday the Russians will take them away to somewhere at the back of the world: so they are seeking comfort.вАЭ

The priest had taken his stand near the poplars, from where he could look through the window, and see how thronged the place was. вАЬWhy, the tavern is well-nigh full!вАЭ he exclaimed.

вАЬThey were to have a meeting and advise together today, about the forest clearing which the Squire has sold to the Jews.вАЭ

вАЬBut he has sold only the half.вАЭ

вАЬTill we have agreed to the sale, not one bush shall be sold!вАЭ

вАЬWhat do you say?вАЭ the priest inquired, in a tone of anxiety.

вАЬWe donвАЩt give leave: thatвАЩs flat. Father would go to law; but Klemba and the others with him wonвАЩt have it. They forbid a single tree to be cut down; and if the whole village has to rise, rise they willвБ†вАФaye, and ax in hand, too. What is theirs, they never will give up.вАЭ

вАЬMerciful heavens! Pray God there may be no violence!вАЭ

вАЬNo, no! only a few of the manor-folksвАЩ heads split in two: that will be but justice!вАЭ

вАЬAntek! has anger made you mad? My good fellow, this is senseless talk!вАЭ

He would not listen, but turned on his heels and vanished in the gathering dusk; while the priest, who heard the rumble of wheels and a mareвАЩs whinny, hastened back to his dwelling.

Antek passed by the mill on the other side, wanting to avoid going near YagnaвАЩs hut.

She was fast in his bosom: a festering wound of which he could not rid himself.

Afar, the light shone bright from within her cabin. In there it was joyful. He stopped to look once more, were it but to curse her in his rage. And suddenly something fell on him like a hurricane, and tore him away.

вАЬShe is my fatherвАЩs now!вБ†вАФMy fatherвАЩs!вАЭ

He went round to his brother-in-law, the smith, though expecting no advice from the man, and only wanting to remain a short time away from his fatherвАЩs dwelling, and in somebodyвАЩs company.вБ†вАФAh! the priest would preach work to him, would he? Preaching to others was an easy thing for those who have nothing to trouble them!вБ†вАФвАЬRemember your wife and child!вАЭвБ†вАФWas he likely to forget them? Her!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ whom he loathed so, with her wailing and her meekness and wistfully glancing eyes! Were it not for herвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ were he but single!вБ†вАФO Lord! He groaned deeply; a wild fit of anger swept over him, and he would have liked to take someone by the throatвБ†вАФstrangle himвБ†вАФtear him to pieces!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

But whom? He knew not. His fury passed away as suddenly as it had come. He looked blankly out into the night and hearkened to the whistling blasts. Then he walked on, trudging heavily, scarce able to drag himself; for now he felt weighed down by a mountain of sorrow, lassitude, and such a sense of prostration that he no longer knew whither he was going, nor for what purpose.

вАЬYagna is my fatherвАЩsвБ†вАФmy fatherвАЩs!вАЭ he repeated again and again, each time in a lower key.

In the smithвАЩs shop, a boy was working the bellows with might and main, and the draught that poured on to the flaring roaring embers made them burst into bloodred flames. The smith stood at the anvil, grimy-faced, girt with a leather apron, his arms bare, his cap on the back of his head, beating a red-hot iron bar till the anvil resounded, while showers of sparks flew from beneath the hammer, and fell hissing into the moist ground of the forge.

вАЬWell?вАЭ he asked, after waiting a moment.

вАЬWell, what?вАЭ Antek mumbled, leaning against a basket-wagon frame, several of which were standing by to have their ironwork repaired; and he gazed into the fire.

The smith went on, working hard at the incandescent iron, and beat away, keeping time as he smote upon the anvil with his hammer; or, when a yet more powerful blast was needed, helping the boy to blow; but ever and anon stealing a glance at Antek, while a malicious smile peeped from under his red moustache.

вАЬWell, so you have been to his Reverence again: and what has come of it?вАЭ

вАЬAnd what should come? Nothing. I might have heard just the same in church.вАЭ

вАЬWhat else did you think to get?вАЭ

вАЬWhy, he knows a great deal,вАЭ Antek replied in self-defence.

вАЬAs to taking, yes; as to giving, no.вАЭ

Antek was in no mood to contradict him.

вАЬI am going to your cabin,вАЭ he said after a pause.

вАЬGo; I shall join you at once, for the Voyt is to be here. You will find tobacco on the top of the press: help yourself.вАЭ

Antek had not so much as heard him, as he made straight for the house which stood opposite.

His sister was kindling the fire, and her eldest boy, at the table, learning out of a spelling-book.

вАЬIs he studying?вАЭ he asked; for the boy spelt aloud, pointing to each letter with a sharp stick.

вАЬYes. He began at potato-digging-time. The young lady from the mill is teaching him, for my husband is too busy.вАЭ

вАЬRoch, too, began teaching on FatherвАЩs side of our cabin yesterday.вАЭ

вАЬI wanted to send our Johnny to him, too: but Michael will not have it. He says she knows more, because she has been at school in Warsaw.вАЭ

вАЬOh, yes. Yes,вАЭ he answered, in order to say something.

вАЬJohnny gets on so fast with his primer that the young lady is astonished.вАЭ

вАЬOh, of course. ItвАЩs the smithвАЩs blood, you seeвБ†вАФbeing the son of so clever a man.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬYou are jeering. And yet was he not right to tell you that Father can, so long as he lives, withdraw any settlement made?вАЭ

вАЬAye, try to snatch its prey from out of the wolfвАЩs mouth!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Six acres of land! My wife and I are both as good as his farm-servants; and see, he settles the land on the first strange woman he comes across!вАЭ

вАЬYou will wrangle, and fall foul of him, and ask for advice against him; and the end will be that he will drive you from his house into the bargain!вАЭ She spoke thus, looking timorously towards the door.

вАЬWho told you that?вАЭ

вАЬHush, hush! ThatвАЩs what people are saying.вАЭ

вАЬHe shall not! Let him get me out by force, if he can! IвАЩll go to law. But as to giving way, never, never!вАЭ

вАЬYes, youвАЩll butt your head against a stone wall, like a ram, but never get it smashed, eh?вАЭ the smith said, coming in.

вАЬThen whatвАЩs to be done? You give clever advice to everybody; advise me.вАЭ

вАЬIt will never do to run counter to the old manвАЩs will.вАЭ He lit a pipe, and set about explaining matters, excusing Boryna, and smoothing things over, till all at once Antek saw his drift, and cried out:

вАЬYouвБ†вАФyou are on his side!вАЭ

вАЬI want to be fair.вАЭ

вАЬYou have been well paid for this.вАЭ

вАЬNot out of your pocket, at all events.вАЭ

вАЬMy property is not yours to give up in my place. You no doubt have had a good instalment already, and are in no hurry to get more.вАЭ

вАЬI have had no more than you.вАЭ

вАЬOh, no more? And what about your share of the cow? And all the pieces of linen, and odds and ends you have sneaked out of Father? Have I forgotten the geese, and the young pigsвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ andвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ andвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ thereвАЩs no end of them! Ah, and the calf he gave you the other day? Is that nothing?вАЭ

вАЬYou might have got it just as well as I.вАЭ

вАЬI am not a gipsy, nor a thief!вАЭ

вАЬA thief! Do you call me that?вАЭ

They both rushed forward, ready to spring at each other. But they stopped, for Antek went on more calmly:

вАЬI was not speaking of you. But never will I abandon my rights, even to be saved from utter ruin.вАЭ

The smith interposed, with a jeer: вАЬIt is not the land, I fancy, for which you would go to such lengths.вАЭ

вАЬFor what then?вАЭ

вАЬIt is Yagna you want, and rage to lose her now!вАЭ

вАЬDid you ever seeвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАК?вАЭ he cried; the shot had hit the mark.

вАЬThere be those who have seenвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ and not once only.вАЭ

вАЬMay their eyes drop out of the sockets!вАЭ But he said this curse very low; for just then the Voyt entered the room. Probably he too was aware of the reason why they quarrelled, for he at once set to justifying and defending the old manвАЩs behaviour.

вАЬThat you stand up for him is no wonder: he has given you drink and sausages in plenty!вАЭ

вАЬNo careless talk, pray; I, the Voyt, am speaking to you.вАЭ

вАЬFor your Voytship I care as I care for this broken stick!вАЭ

вАЬWhat!вБ†вАФwhat has the man said?вАЭ

вАЬYou have heard; and if not, you shall hear other things which will go yet farther.вАЭ

вАЬSay them, then, if you dare!вАЭ

вАЬI will.вБ†вАФBehold, you are a drunkard, a Judas, a dissembler; one that squanders in revels the money the village has entrusted to him, and takes abundant pay from the manor, to let the Squire sell our forest land.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Will ye I say more?вАЭ he added furiously, snatching at a stick. вАЬSo I will, but with this cudgel, not with my tongue.вАЭ

вАЬTake care you rue not what you are doing, Antek; I am a man in office!вАЭ

вАЬAnd do not fly at anyone under my roof! This is no tavern!вАЭ the smith shouted, placing himself in front of the Voyt. But Antek, now wrought up to exasperation, poured a volley of abuse on them both, slammed the door, and left them.

вАЬNow,вАЭ he was saying to himself, while breakfasting the next day, вАЬnow they will all be against me!вАЭ when, to his stupefaction, he saw the blacksmith come in. They met on their usual terms.

When Antek went to the barn afterwards to chop straw, the smith followed him, and said in confidential tones:

вАЬIвАЩll be hanged if I know why we quarrelledвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ some silly word dropped, belike. So I am first to come to you and shake hands.вАЭ

Antek shook hands indeed, but grunted, with a look of mistrust:

вАЬYes, some hasty words passed between us; but I felt no grudge against you. That Voyt made me frantic.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Let him mind his own business, and keep himself to himself, or.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬSo I told him, when he wanted to follow you out.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬTo fight me?вБ†вАФI would have given him such a dressing as I gave his cousin, who has been smashed up ever since harvest-time!вАЭ

вАЬOf that, too, did I remind him,вАЭ the smith observed, with a demure look and a sly leer.

вАЬBut I will settle with him yetвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ with that great man, that Jack in office! He will remember me!вАЭ

вАЬHe is not worth your notice: let him be.вБ†вАФI have had an idea, and have come now to tell you about it. This is what we have to do.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ This afternoon my wife will come here. You will go with her to old Boryna, and talk the matter over thoroughly.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Of what use is complaining in holes and corners? Speak your mind out to him face to face. Perhaps you will succeed, perhaps not; but at all events we shall have threshed the matter out.вАЭ

вАЬBut what is to be done, now the settlement has been made?вАЭ

вАЬYou see, by wrangling we shall get nothing at all. Yes, he has made it. But, so long as he lives, he has the power to revoke it. Do you understand? That is the reason why we must not irritate him. He wants to marry: well, let him. And to enjoy himself: why not?вАЭ

At the mention of marriage, Antek turned white, and shook so that he paused in his work.

вАЬDo not oppose him openly. Approve him. Say he was right to make the settlement, since he chose to do so: only ask him to promise us the restвБ†вАФthat is, to you and me, and in presence of witnesses,вАЭ he added, with a sly afterthought.

вАЬYes, but what of Yuzka, what of Gregory?вАЭ Antek inquired reluctantly.

вАЬThey shall get money instead. Gregory has been receiving not a little every month, ever since he has been in the army.вБ†вАФBut just listen, and do as I tell you; you will not regret it. My management of things will make all the land ours in the end, my life on it.вАЭ

вАЬвАКвАШTo sew the sheepвАЩs skin do not strive, furrier, while the sheepвАЩs alive.вАЩвАКвАЭ

вАЬListenвБ†вАФLet him but make a promise in presence of witnesses: we shall then have something to lay hold on. We can still fall back on the courts of justice. And there is another point besides: the land he got as your motherвАЩs dowry.вАЭ

вАЬA great thing, forsooth: four acres for me and your sisterвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ four whole acres!вАЭ

вАЬBut these he has not given to either; and for so many years he has sown therein and garnered therefrom! For these he must pay you well, aye, and with percentage too!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I tell you once more: oppose the old man in nothing. Go to the wedding; do not grudge him fair words. We shall manage him, you will see. And if he is after all unwilling to give the promise, the law may then come in and force him. You are on very familiar terms with Yagna, and she may be very useful to you: only speak of this to her. No one could better succeed in bringing the old man round.вБ†вАФWell, is it agreed? For I must be stirring.вАЭ

вАЬAgreed!вБ†вАФThat you get out quick, or I will smite you in the face and drive you out of doors!вАЭ Antek hissed through his clenched teeth.

вАЬWhatвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ what has come over you?вАЭ the blacksmith stammered, appalled by the looks of the other, who dropped the straw-cutter and came on, with eyes terribly gleaming and face as pale as a sheet.

вАЬThief! carrion! traitor!вАЭ He spat the words out, his mouth was foaming with hate as he advanced, and the smith fairly ran for it.

вАЬHas the man lost his wits?вАЭ he said, as soon as he was out in the road. вАЬI was giving him good counselвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ and heвБ†вАФOh, thatвАЩs your game, is it? You would have struck me, driven me out, because I wanted to share the land with you, and came to you as to a friend and a brother! Is that your gameвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ to have all to yourself? Ha! you will not live to see the day, my man! Though you wormed my thoughts out of me so cleverly, I will give you such a shaking, the worst ague will be nothing beside it!вАЭ He grew angrier and still more angry, as he reflected that Antek had taken him in so, and would inform old Boryna of all this intrigue.вБ†вАФThe very thing he feared most of all!

вАЬBut that must at once be prevented!вАЭ He swiftly came to a decision, and though in bodily fear of Antek, went back to BorynaвАЩs.

вАЬIs your master at home?вАЭ he asked Vitek, who was opposite the house, throwing pebbles at the geese in the pond to make them land.

вАЬOver there at the millerвАЩs: gone to invite their people to his wedding.вАЭ

вАЬI shall go that way: perhaps we may meet,вАЭ he thought, and made for the millerвАЩs; but he went home first, and told his wife to dress her best, take the children with her, and go round to AntekвАЩs at the first stroke of the noonday Angelus.

вАЬHe will tell you what to do.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Do nothing by yourself, for you are not clever; only fall a-crying at the right time, embrace your fatherвАЩs knees and beseech him, and all that. But give good heed to what Antek shall say and your father reply.вАЭ And so he went on instructing her for some time.

вАЬNow I shall look in at the mill: perhaps our meal is ground.вАЭ He was too uneasy to stay any longer in the house and, going out, walked on slowly, often halting to consider.

вАЬThe man threatened me; yet heвАЩll do as I told him, I think. Better my wife should be there, and not I.вБ†вАФWhat else can he do but what I say?вБ†вАФQuarrelвБ†вАФand be expelled!вАЭ

He smiled in triumph, set his cap straight and buttoned up his capote, for a chill piercing wind came from the pond.

вАЬThere will be frost, surely, or else dirty weather,вАЭ he predicted, standing on the bridge and looking into the sky, where a scud of driven clouds was passing, not unlike a flock of muddy unwashed sheep. The pond uttered a low murmur, now and then beating upon its shores, along which, scattered about amongst blackened drooping alders and weeping willows, the outlines of women washing linen appeared, traced in red, and the obstreperous clatter of their bats rose on either bank. The roads were empty, save for the numerous flocks of geese, soiled with stiffening mire, that were waddling in and out of the ditches, now filled up with dead leaves and rubbish. Children outside the houses squealed and screamed; and the cocks crowed in the hedgesвБ†вАФweather-prophets telling of a change.

вАЬBetter wait for him at the mill!вАЭ he growled and walked down the slope.

Antek, when the smith left him, had set to chopping straw so frantically that he forgot everything but his work; and Kuba, returning from the wood, cried out aloud:

вАЬMercy! there will be enough of it for a weekвАЩs fodder!вАЭ And then Antek woke up from his musings, threw the straw-cutter aside, stretched himself, and went into the hut.

вАЬWhat must be will be,вАЭ he reflected, вАЬand I must speak to my father this day.вБ†вАФThat blacksmith fellow is a lying traitor; his advice may be good, for all that. Nay, there must be something in it.вАЭ He peeped in at his fatherвАЩs door, and at once drew back; a score of urchins were sitting there. Roch was teaching them, and paying great heed to their behaviour; going round with beads in hand, hearing their lessons, correcting them at times; at others pulling one boyвАЩs ear or patting anotherвАЩs head, but for the most part sitting patiently and explaining the printed matter, or putting questions, which the children hastened to answer in chorus as fast as they could, gobbling like a troop of little turkeys when excited.

Hanka was getting dinner ready, and having a talk with her father, old Bylitsa, who seldom came, because he was always ailing and could hardly move about.

He sat close to the window, his chin and hands on his staff; hoary-headed, with a twitch of the lips and a treble voice like a birdвАЩs, accompanied by thin wheezing sounds in the windpipe.

вАЬHave you breakfasted?вАЭ she inquired.

вАЬTo say true, Veronka forgot me.вАЭ

вАЬOh, she even starves her dogs! they often come to me for food,вАЭ she cried. Her elder sister and she had been on bad terms ever since last winter, when their mother had died, and Veronka seized on all she had left, refusing to give anything up; which had estranged them.

He took her part in a feeble voice. вАЬThey have not too much for themselves. Staho threshes at the organistвАЩs, where he gets food and a score of kopeks daily besides. And there are many mouths to feed in the cabin: the potato-patch cannot suffice for all. True, they have a couple of milch-cows and take butter and cheese to town, and get a few coppers; but she often forgets to give me my meals. Yet I do not want muchвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ only a little every day, and at the right hour.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬThen come to us in spring, since you are so ill off with that jade!вАЭ

вАЬBut I make no complaint, no fuss; only.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ His voice died into silence.

вАЬWith us, you could tend the geese, and see to the children.вАЭ

вАЬHanka,вАЭ he said under his breath, вАЬthere is nothing that I would not do.вАЭ

вАЬThere is room for you here; I should put up a bed for you and make you cosy.вАЭ

вАЬOh, if I could but be with you, Hanka, and never go back to them, I would sleep in the cow-house or the stable,вАЭ he answered in a husky beseeching voice. вАЬThey took my featherbed from me; she says the children have nothing to sleep on. It is true that they were cold, so I had them with me. But my sheepskin is all torn, and does not keep me warm at all; and where I sleep there is no fire, and she will not let me have any wood, and counts every spoonful that I eat, and sends me out a-begging, and I am so weak I can scarcely crawl to your house.вАЭ

вАЬGood God! and you never told me this was so!вБ†вАФWhy?вАЭ

вАЬHow could I? she is my daughter!вБ†вАФAnd he is a good-hearted man, but very little in the house.вБ†вАФHow could I?вАЭ

вАЬShe is a hag! She took half the land and half the cabin, and the other things.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ So thatвАЩs the board and lodgings she promised to give you! We must go to law: they were bound to let you have food and firing, and clothing too.вБ†вАФAnd we were to give twelve roubles a year: have we not kept our promise, say?вАЭ

вАЬSurely! For you are upright folk.вБ†вАФBut those few zloty that I have saved for my burialвБ†вАФI had to give them up too, I could not help it.вАЭ He said no more but sat crouching in his place, more like a heap of rags than a human being.

After dinner, when the smithвАЩs wife came with her children and greeted Hanka, the old man took up a bundle prepared for him by his daughter, and vanished unnoticed.

Boryna had not come home to dine.

The smithвАЩs wife was determined to see him, nevertheless, though she should have to wait till nightfall. Hanka had set up a loom near the window, where she set to work, drawing the woof of hempen thread across the warp assiduously, and but seldom and timidly taking part in the talk between Antek and his sister. His conversation with her about their grievances did not last long, however; for Yagustynka dropped in, saying in a casual tone:

вАЬI have just come here from the organistвАЩs, where they need me for the washing. Matthias was there only just now, together with Yagna, to invite them to the wedding. They are coming. Yes, everyone to his people: the rich to the rich. They have asked the priest also.вАЭ

вАЬWhat! have they dared His Reverence!вАЭ Hanka exclaimed.

вАЬIs he, then, so sacred a being? They asked him, and he said he might possibly come. Why not? Is the girl ill-looking? will the food be bad? and will there be little to drink? The miller and wife and daughter have promised. Ho, ho! There will not have been such a wedding since Lipka was Lipka!вБ†вАФI know, for I shall be cooking with EvaвБ†вАФher from the millerвАЩs. Ambrose has killed a pig for them, and sausages are making nowвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ She broke off abruptly, noticing that no one asked any questions, or spoke at all. She looked round at them as they sat gloomily there, and, eyeing them attentively, cried out:

вАЬI say! there is a storm brewing here!вАЭ

вАЬStorm or no storm, what is that to you?вАЭ the smithвАЩs wife answered, so tartly that Yagustynka was offended, rose, and went over to Yuzka in the other lodgings, who (the children having just departed) was setting chairs and benches in order.

вАЬFather is not likely to grudge himself anything,вАЭ the smithвАЩs wife remarked, in an aggrieved tone.

вАЬOh, he can well afford it!вАЭ Hanka rejoined, and broke off abruptly, seeing Antek look fiercely at her.вБ†вАФThey sat waiting in almost complete silence. From time to time a word was said; then that dull, crushing, ominous speechlessness came over them once more.

вАЬHe must have cash enough: he is always selling things, and never spending.вАЭ

AntekвАЩs only rejoinder to his sisterвАЩs words was a wave of the hand; and he went out of the room to get some fresh air. He was feeling ever more and more uneasy; nor could he tell why. He now expected his father, and felt impatient at the delay, yet glad in his heart not to have met with him yet.вБ†вАФвАЬIt is not the land you are angry about, it is Yagna!вАЭвБ†вАФThose words, uttered by the smith the day before, now suddenly came back to him.вБ†вАФвАЬHe is a lying dog!вАЭ was the cry of rage which burst from his lips. And he set to work at the outer wall which was to protect the hut from the side of the courtyard. Vitek brought him litter from the heap; Antek drove in the laths to form the wall, and rammed the litter down inside it; but his hands were trembling, he had to stop working more than once, and lean against the cabin walls, and look out through the bare leafless trees over the pond to YagnaвАЩs hut.вБ†вАФNo, it was not love that was now growing within him, but anger and hatred in numberless billows! She, the jadeвБ†вАФshe, the hateful one!вБ†вАФThey had thrown her a bone, and off she went after it!

Such were his thoughts. But then there swept over him remembrances coming upвБ†вАФwhence, he knew notвБ†вАФlaying siege to his heart, clinging to his mind, even visible to his sensesвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ and the sweat bedewed his brow, his eyes flashed, a thrill ran through him.вБ†вАФAh, there in the orchard! Ah, then in the forest! And again, when they once were coming from town together!

All at once he reeled; he again saw that burning face, those deep-blue eyes, those wondrous full red lips; and he heard her quick-drawn breaths of passion, and her voice, low and husky with love and rapture, calling to him: вАЬAntek! Antek!вАЭ And she was again bending towards him, very closeвБ†вАФhe felt her touch him with all her throbbing self!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ But he rubbed his eyes to drive away that too sweet phantom, and his implacable resentment again oozed icily from his heart, as the drops fall from the icicles under the eaves, when the spring sun shines upon them, and love awakens once more; within his soul, agonized yearning lifted her thorn-crowned head once moreвБ†вАФa yearning so bitter that he would fain have eased it by clutching at any pain whatsoever, or by shrieking to rouse the dead!

вАЬMay a brimstone thunderbolt strike her!вАЭ he cried out; but, suddenly recollecting himself, he cast a sharp glance round, fearing lest Vitek should have understood whom he meant.

He had spent those three last weeks in a fever of expectancy, awaiting the happening of some miracle. As for him, he could do nothing, prevent nothing!

And of late, insane thoughts had often surged up in his mind, insane resolves. Often had he gone out to meet her, and many a night had he watched outside her cabin, in the rain and the cold. But she had not come out.вБ†вАФShe shunned him!

No, no, no! Every instant he grew more angry against her, against the whole framework of things. She was his fatherвАЩs!вБ†вАФA strange woman, an adventuress, a thief who had robbed him of his land, the most precious of all possessions! Smite her he wouldвБ†вАФaye, beat the life out of her!

More than once he had determined to confront his father, and tell him to his face: вАЬYou cannot have Yagna; she is mine!вАЭ But the very thought made his hair stand on end.вБ†вАФWhat would his father, what would all the village say?

So now she, that same Yagna, was to be his stepmotherвБ†вАФhis motherвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ of a sort! How could that be? Was it not a sin, a most grievous one? He was afraid to think of it: the thought of some awful judgment of God at hand made his heart die within him.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ And yet, to say nothingвБ†вАФto bear all this within himself, as one bearing in his bosom coals of fire that burned to the boneвБ†вАФthat was beyond the endurance of man!

And the wedding was but a week away!

вАЬMaster is coming,вАЭ Vitek cried; and Antek felt he was shaking with dismay.

It was getting dark.

It was getting cold, too; the ground was freezing, the air eager and nipping, but clear as usual when a frost is setting in, and wafting sound so well that the bellowing and trampling of the cattle driven to water, the creaking of the gates and bucket-dippers, the noises of the children and the dogs, were all heard distinctly across the pond. From some windows, there gleamed lights already, throwing athwart the waters their long, broken, quivering reflections; while, from behind the woods, the huge red full moon was slowly ascending.

Boryna, attentive to farm matters, came into the yard, and rated Kuba and Vitek soundly for having let the calves stray from their stalls and wander to the cowsвАЩ mangers; so, when he entered the house, his visitors were awaiting him. They said nothing, but just gave one glance, and looked down, as he stopped short in the middle of the room, eyed them, and asked scornfully:

вАЬAll here? What, come to sit in judgment, hey?вАЭ

вАЬNo, indeed,вАЭ the smithвАЩs wife returned, timorously; вАЬwe only come to you with a petition.вАЭ

вАЬBut why is your goodman not here?вАЭ

вАЬHe was very busy, and could not come.вАЭ

вАЬAha! BusyвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ yes.вАЭ He smiled knowingly, threw his capote aside, and pulled off his boots. All remained tongue-tied the while, uncertain how to begin. The smithвАЩs wife cleared her throat and drew her children closer; Hanka, on the threshold, was suckling her little boy, and casting uneasy looks at Antek, who sat by the window thinking what he should say, and shaking all over with emotion. Yuzka alone was calm, peeling potatoes by the fireplace.

вАЬNow, then, say what you have to say,вАЭ the old man cried sharply, irritated by the silence.

вАЬBetter you, Antek, should speak firstвБ†вАФabout that settlement: we shall follow,вАЭ the smithвАЩs wife stammered.

вАЬThe settlement? It is made, and the wedding is to be on Sunday: that I can tell you.вАЭ

вАЬWe know, but we came for another reason.вАЭ

вАЬWhat is it?вАЭ

вАЬYou have settled six whole acres!вАЭ

вАЬI chose to: if I choose, I can settle everything on her, and this instant!вАЭ

вАЬYou may, if all belongs to you,вАЭ Antek retorted.

вАЬAnd whose else is itвБ†вАФwhose?вАЭ

вАЬYour childrenвАЩs. Ours.вАЭ

вАЬThatвАЩs nonsense. Mine the land is, and I can do with it as I please.вАЭ

вАЬOr not yours, and not to do as you please.вАЭ

вАЬWill you prevent meвБ†вАФyou?вАЭ

вАЬI shallвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ we all shall; and if not, we have the law to protect us.вАЭ He could no longer control himself, and was raging.

вАЬAh! you do threaten me with the law, forsooth?вБ†вАФHold your peace ere I am angered, or youвАЩll rue it.вАЭ

вАЬWrong us ye shall not!вАЭ cried Hanka in a loud voice, rising to her feet.

вАЬAnd what isвАЩt she wantsвБ†вАФshe?вБ†вАФShe brought us three acres of sand, and one piece of canvas cloth: and she dares wag her tongue here!вАЭ

вАЬYou have given Antek still less: not even the land, his motherвАЩs dowry; we are as your farm-labourers!вАЭ

вАЬBut in return for your work you get all that three of my acres yield.вАЭ

вАЬFor work that is worth the yield of more than twenty.вАЭ

вАЬIf unfairly treated, go elsewhere and fare better.вАЭ

Here Antek shouted: вАЬWe will not! The land is ours, come down from our grandsires and forefathers.вАЭ

Old Boryna glared at him, but answered nothing. He seated himself by the fire and, taking up a poker, used it on the brands till the sparks flew on every side. He was flushed with passion; his hair again and again came tumbling into his eyes, phosphorescent like a wildcatвАЩs; but he had some self-control still left.

A long pause ensued, and the stillness of the room was broken only by the hurried breaths drawn there.

вАЬWe have naught against your marrying; marry, if you like.вАЭ

вАЬAnd if you have aught, much difference will it make to me!вАЭ

вАЬOnly revoke that settlement!вАЭ added Hanka, in tears.

вАЬOh, that peevish mother of dogs! Always chattering like a fool!вАЭ And he poked the fire so furiously that the sparks flew all about the room.

вАЬTake heed! She is no wench of yours, that you should speak such words to her!вАЭ

вАЬWhy should she prate, then?вАЭ

вАЬShe has a right to speak!вАЭ Antek shouted; вАЬshe stands up for what is our due.вАЭ

вАЬIf you will,вАЭ the smithвАЩs wife murmured, вАЬlet the settlement stand, but settle the rest of your property on us.вАЭ

вАЬLook at that simpleton! Going to divide my land, eh? No, IвАЩll never take board and lodgings from you.вБ†вАФI have spoken.вАЭ

вАЬWe will not give in! We will have justice!вАЭ

вАЬIf I but take my stick to you, IвАЩll give you justice!вАЭ

вАЬTry but to touch us!вБ†вАФYouвАЩd not live till the wedding!вАЭ

And now the squabble began in earnest; they rushed forwards, threatening; they beat the table with their fists, they shouted aloud all their grievances, all their injuries. Antek, in his anger, forgot himself so far as again and again to clutch his father by the shoulder, even by the throat, so furious was he; but the old man was yet master of himself. He wished to have no fight, and merely pushed him aside, seldom replying to insults, and unwilling to have the whole village taking part in his affairs. But the noise and confusion in the room waxed louder and louder; for both the women were weeping and pouring forth invectives alternately, while the children screamed so that both Kuba and Vitek came round from the farmyard and peeped in at the window.

Hanka, leaning against the chimney penthouse, here burst into a torrent of tears and words:

вАЬYes, we shall have to go out into the world and beg our bread! O Lord, good Lord!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ we that have toiled like oxen!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ What have we now of our labour?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Ah, God will avenge this wrong of ours!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ His judgment will be upon you!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Six whole acres settledвБ†вАФand motherвАЩs clothing and beads given awayвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ everything! And to whom, great God?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ To that swine!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Oh! wanton and harlot as you are! For the wrong you are doing us, may you end in a ditch some day!вАЭ

вАЬWhat do you say?вАЭ the old man shrieked, darting furiously towards her.

вАЬThat she is a harlot and a wantonвБ†вАФas all the village and all the world knows!вАЭ

вАЬWoe betide you! IвАЩll beat your foul mouth to pulp!вАЭ He seized and shook her; but Antek leaped forwards to protect her, and shouted in his turn:

вАЬAnd I say it too: she is a wanton, a harlot, and anyone may know her that cares!вАЭвБ†вАФBut he said no more. Boryna, in a paroxysm of rage, struck him such a blow in the face that he fell with his head breaking the pane of a glazed press, which he brought to the floor with him. Springing up instantly, streaming with blood, he charged his father.

They both rushed at each other like mad dogs, with a mutual clutch, driving and being driven backward and forward about the room, pushing and hurling one another against the bed, the great trunk, the walls, till their heads rang again. A horrible outcry arose: the womenfolk tried to separate them, but they rolled down upon the floor, so closely gripped in hatred that they turned over and over, each strangling each, each crushing the other, as best he could.

By great good fortune, the neighbours ran in while it was time, and separated them.

Antek was hustled away to the other lodgings, and water dashed over him; he was faint with exhaustion caused by loss of blood, for the glass had gashed him very deep.

The old man had no hurt at all; only a slight tear in the short jacket he wore, and a few scratches on his face, that was livid with rage.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ He swore at the folk who had come, shut the front door on them, and sat down by the fire.

But nothing could avail to calm him.

He could not put out of his memory the words uttered about Yagna: they stabbed him like a knife.

вАЬThat hound! I will never forgive him, never!вАЭ was the oath he then swore to himself. вАЬMy Yagna! how could he?вАЭвБ†вАФBut then he recalled what he had heard said of her in former times and disregarded. He turned hot, he felt as if he were choking, and a wretched sense of dejection came over him. How, if his own son said such things, were peopleвАЩs mouths to be stopped? Oh, that villain! The very recollection of those words burned him like fire.

After Yuzka had cleaned away all the traces of the struggle, and given him his supper, though late, he attempted to eat, but could not, and laid his spoon down. вАЬHave you given the horses their provender?вАЭ he inquired of Kuba.

вАЬOf course.вАЭ

вАЬVitekвБ†вАФwhere is he?вАЭ

вАЬGone for Ambrose, to see to AntekвАЩs head. His face is swollen like a pipkin,вАЭ he added, hurrying out; for he had chosen this moonlight night to go out shooting.

вАЬвАКвАШWhen dogs have too much bread, each flies at tвАЩotherвАЩs head,вАЩвАКвАЭ he grunted.

The old man stumped down into the village, but refrained from visiting Yagna, though the light was gleaming bright from her window. He turned away just outside her door, and went round to the mill. It was a chilly star-besprinkled night, and so clear that the whole millpond shone like glittering quicksilver. Over the deserted roads the trees cast long swaying shadows. It was late; they were putting the lights out in the houses, whose whitewashed walls now stood out more distinctly among the skeleton orchard trees. Silence and darkness had swallowed up all the hamlet: only the mill-wheel and the water clattered and babbled monotonously. Matthias walked on, crossing to the other side. As he went, his anger grew stronger, together with his hatred. When he got to the tavern, he sent for the Voyt, and they both drank till midnight. He could not, however, drown the gnawing pain within him. Only he then registered a resolve.

No sooner had he risen the next morning than he went round to the other lodgings. Antek was in bed, his face bandaged with a bloodstained rag.

вАЬGet out of my home this instant!вАЭ he said, вАЬand let no trace of you remain! If you want war, if you will go to law, then do so; bring an action, and get back your property! What you have sown of your own grain, you may reap, when summer comes. And now, away with you! Let me set eyes on you no more! Do you hear?вАЭ he roared. Antek set about dressing slowly.

вАЬBy noon, you will have to be off!вАЭ he added, calling out to them from the passage.

Antek remained as dumb as though he had not heard.

вАЬYuzka, call Kuba: let him put the mare to the cart, and take them whither they want to go!вАЭ

вАЬBut there is something the matter with Kuba. He lies groaning on his pallet, and says he cannot rise at all, his lame leg hurts him so.вАЭ

вАЬA sluggard, who only wants to lie abed!вАЭ And Boryna saw to the farm-duties by himself.

Kuba nevertheless was seriously ill, but would not say what the matter was with him, though pressed by his master. As he lay, he uttered such groans that the horses came up to him, sniffed at his face and licked it, while Vitek brought him water in a pail, and secretly washed certain blood-smirched rags in the river.

Boryna, intent as he was on the departure of Antek and his family, noticed nothing of all this.

They departed.

Without clamour or disturbance, they packed everything, carried their belongings out, and made up their bundles; Hanka well-nigh swooning with distress; Antek refreshing her with drinks of water and hurrying her on, that they might be awayвБ†вАФout of that fatherвАЩs houseвБ†вАФas quickly as ever they could.

He would take no horse from his father, but borrowed one from Klemba, and took everything over to HankaвАЩs parent, at the very end of the village and beyond the tavern.

Several peasants had come in from the hamlet, along with Roch as their leader, desirous of reconciling them; but to this neither father nor son would agree.

вАЬNo,вАЭ said the old man; вАЬlet him try how he will enjoy his freedom, and bread of his own!вАЭ

Antek answered no word to their solicitations; but, lifting his fist, he uttered such horrible maledictions that Roch turned pale and withdrew amongst the women, who were in numbers about the premises; partly to assist Hanka, but for the most part to air grievances aloud, and babble, and give advice.

When Yuzka, all in tears, gave dinner to her father and Roch, her brother and his family were off the place, together with all they had. Antek never even looked back at his hut; he only crossed himself, heaving a deep sigh; and whipping up the horse, put his shoulder to the cart, it being very heavily laden. He went plodding along, his face white, his eyes blazing with stubborn resolve, his teeth chattering as one in an ague: but never said one word. Hanka walked languidly after the cart, her elder son holding to her skirt and roaring, her younger one clasped to her bosom. Before them she drove a cow, a flock of geese, and two lean swine: and her voice was so loud in imprecations and mourning that folk came out of their houses, and followed her as in procession.

At BorynaвАЩs, the meal was eaten in sombre silence.

The old dog Lapa barked in the porch, ran after the cart, returned and howled. Vitek called it; but it paid no heed. It smelt the farmyard, entered AntekвАЩs empty rooms, ran round them one or twice, rushed into the passage, barked again, whined, fawned on Yuzka, and again tore about as though distracted: then it sat down on its hind quarters with a strange air of imbecilityвБ†вАФand finally made off, with its tail between its legs, on AntekвАЩs trail.

вАЬEven Lapa has gone after them!вАЭ

вАЬDo not fear, Yuzka,вАЭ her father answered tenderly; вАЬLapa is coming back soon. They will have no food for him. Come, no silly puling, but prepare the other rooms: Roch is to live in them. Call Yagustynka to help you.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ You must take household matters in hand now; being housekeeper, youвАЩll have many a care on your head.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ No, no! no whimpering, dear!вАЭ He took her head in both his hands, and stroked it, and drew her caressingly to his heart.

вАЬWhen I go to town, IвАЩll buy you a pair of shoes.вАЭ

вАЬOh, will you, will you, Father?вАЭ

вАЬYes, I will indeed, and many another thing besides. Only be a good girl, and take care of the place.вАЭ

вАЬAnd will you buy me a caftan like NastusiaвАЩs?вАЭ

вАЬCertainly, dear, IвАЩll buy you one.вАЭ

вАЬAnd ribbons too?вБ†вАФBut long onesвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ such as I shall want for your wedding-day.вАЭ

вАЬSay but what you need, little one, and you shall have itвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ all you want!вАЭ
